definition: Lasting for a very short time
example: The ephemeral beauty of the sunset faded quickly.

definition: Past tense of 'stride' — to walk with long, confident steps
example: He strode in a swarm of fireflies.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: To move gently through the air, carried by a light current
example: Whistling, he let the escalator waft him into the still night air.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A small metal cap worn on a fingertip to push a needle; here used metaphorically for tiny earpiece radios
example: The thimble radios tamped tight.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Bradbury's invented word combining 'thimble' (tiny earpiece) + 'wasps' (buzzing insects) — the buzzing sound of the ear radios
example: There was only the singing of the thimblewasps in her tamped-shut ears.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Packed or plugged tightly closed, sealed
example: Tamped-shut ears.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Flashed or gleamed with a small reflected light
example: The object he sent tumbling with his foot now glinted under the edge of his own bed.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A highly venomous African snake known for puffing up when threatened; used here as a metaphor for dangerous, cold-eyed men
example: The men with the eyes of puff-adders.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A small medicinal tablet, often diamond-shaped, meant to dissolve slowly in the mouth
example: And let a sleep-lozenge dissolve on his tongue.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A shelter or housing structure for a dog; here used for the mechanical Hound's storage compartment
example: Softly illuminated kennel back in a dark corner of the firehouse.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: To grab or capture something suddenly and forcefully
example: There would be betting to see which the Hound would seize first.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: The elongated snout or tube-like appendage of an animal (e.g. elephant trunk, insect mouthpart); here the Hound's needle
example: From the proboscis of the Hound.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A local anaesthetic drug used to numb pain, similar to novocaine
example: Massive jolts of morphine or procaine.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Loud cries or shouts of excitement and laughter
example: Listening to whoops of laughter.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A metal grating or grid used as a cover or screen
example: Hidden behind the grille.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Made feverish; intensely excited or agitated, as if with a fever
example: Work flushed their cheeks and fevered their eyes.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Crushing or scrunching something so it folds and wrinkles
example: Crumpling the cellophane into a sound of fire.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A thin, transparent wrapping material that crinkles when touched
example: Crumpling the cellophane into a sound of fire.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Extremely unpleasant or repulsive; deserving strong dislike
example: Suddenly odious and fat in the plump fireproof stickers.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: To move or act in a carefree, boisterous, jolly way
example: Through like boys all rollick and shout.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: On that thing; on it (formal/archaic)
example: The words delicately painted thereon.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Units of depth (1 fathom = ~1.8m), used figuratively here for great depth or darkness
example: Staring at the fathoms of blackness above her in the ceiling.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Bradbury's invented compound word — audio-visual wall screens combining sound (phono) and colour
example: Between the slots of the phono-colour walls.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: To act out or communicate using gestures and expressions without speaking
example: He could only pantomime.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A living room or sitting room; in the novel, the room dominated by the interactive TV walls
example: He heard the 'relatives' shouting in the parlour.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Having a healthy reddish complexion
example: With a peaceful look on his ruddy face.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A fortified wine ranging from pale gold to dark brown in colour
example: The walls like confetti or blood or sherry or sauterne.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A sweet golden French white wine; here evoking a rich amber colour
example: The walls like confetti or blood or sherry or sauterne.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A starchy food made from cassava, usually white and pudding-like in texture
example: A nice blend of vanilla tapioca.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A wall or embankment built to hold back water; used here metaphorically for holding back a flood of change
example: We have our fingers in the dyke.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Heavy and dull like lead; oppressive, without energy or hope
example: (Context forgotten — page unknown)
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A utensil with a mesh used to separate fine particles from coarse ones; used metaphorically for a leaky memory
example: Trying to fill a sieve with sand.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A piece of clothing
example: Stitched the patches of the universe together into one garment for us.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Relating to the Praetorian Guard — the elite soldiers protecting Roman emperors; used here for enforcers of power
example: They're Caesar's praetorian guard.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A very small amount; a little bit
example: His lips moving just a trifle.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Sweat; liquid secreted through the skin when hot or anxious
example: The perspiration gathered with the silence.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: To throw or lift something with great effort
example: You heave them into the 'parlour' and turn the switch.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Laughed in a nervous, restrained, or silly way
example: Mrs Bowles tittered.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A belt or band worn around the waist; here used poetically for a glowing strip of light around the world
example: Lay like the folds of a bright girdle furled.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Rolled or folded up tightly
example: Lay like the folds of a bright girdle furled.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Dreary; bleak, dull, and cheerless (archaic/poetic form of 'dreary')
example: Down the vast edges drear.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Flat pieces of stone or pebbles on a beach; also roof tiles
example: And naked shingles of the world.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Cooked gently just below boiling; metaphorically: developed slowly over time
example: After everything had mixed and simmered.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Delicate ornamental work made of fine wire; used here as a metaphor for something intricate and fine
example: Delicate filigree murmur of the old man's voice.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A communal toilet, often in a military or outdoor setting
example: Go to the latrine to wash his hands.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: An open, wild, often boggy landscape covered with heather
example: Lost in the middle of the moor.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Showing great happiness, as if blessed; blissfully joyful
example: We drove back to the firehouse in beatific silence.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Done hastily and without care, just to get it done
example: He glanced perfunctorily at it.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Small pieces of partially burned material; ash and ember remains
example: It bedded itself down in sleepy pink-grey cinders.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Dressed or covered in something
example: Clad it in a new covering as it slammed into Montag.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: The trunk of a tree
example: And threw him ten feet back against the bole of a tree.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A deep ditch filled with water surrounding a castle or fortification
example: Beyond the tin moat of the city.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: A fragrant spice with a warm, sweet-spicy aroma, used in cooking and perfumery
example: All cardamon and moss and ragweed odour.
author: Ray Bradbury

definition: Moving quickly with short, hurried steps, like a small animal
example: A rat came scuttling down the slope.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: A type of tall evergreen conifer tree
example: A group of crooked fir trees.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: In an irritable, grumpy, or sulky manner
example: The rat continued peevishly.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: In folklore, a small helpful household spirit or creature from British and Scottish tradition
example: She was still a young brownie.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Lazy people who do nothing useful; idlers
example: Sharp-tongued brownie layabouts.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Chatting constantly and at length about unimportant things
example: She just keeps nattering on.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Trembling or quivering, vibrating with excitement or nervousness
example: Her whiskers were all a-quiver.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Ran quickly with light, hurried steps
example: The rat scampered nimbly up the rock.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: In a quick, light, and agile manner
example: The rat scampered nimbly up the rock.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: A soft, dry, crackling sound made by something moving gently, like leaves or animals in undergrowth
example: The rustle of foxes out hunting by night.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Sat or balanced on something narrow or elevated
example: Sorrel perched on his back.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Solidly built; fat and sturdy
example: Said a large, stout dragon scornfully.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: A mild exclamation of annoyance or frustration
example: Oh, drat it!
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: A small, choice piece of food; a tasty morsel (British spelling of 'tidbit')
example: Started searching the wet grass for her titbit.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: In a low, slightly hoarse voice, often suggesting emotion or tiredness
example: Then he said, huskily.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Walked slowly and with heavy steps, as if tired or struggling
example: Slatebeard trudged back to his cave.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Arguing noisily about something trivial
example: Do stop squabbling.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Climbed awkwardly using both hands and feet
example: Sorrel mumbled with her mouth full as she clambered up.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: A type of rough plaster coating applied to the outside of walls
example: Got a handhold on the roughcast wall.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Informal British term for a very small person
example: A little titch like you isn't usually out.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: The triangular upper parts of a wall at the end of a ridged roof
example: With tall windows and pointed gables.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: A railing supported by a row of short posts, typically along a staircase, bridge, or balcony
example: Clutching the balustrade of the bridge.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: Slightly open
example: She stopped in front of a brown door that was slightly ajar.
author: Cornelia Funke

definition: A large feather from a bird's wing or tail, especially when used as a writing pen
example: A bird's feather with its quill sharpened lay beside them.
author: Cornelia Funke
